cot_prefix: Okay, let's analyze the question and the passage. The question asks why the text uses a lot of figures (numbers). The answer choices are about the purpose of using those figures. Let me go through the passage again and identify where the figures are used and what their purpose is: 1. "about 30 percent of Chinese urban households now own at least one credit card" - This is a statistic that provides evidence about credit card ownership. 2. "the growth rate of credit card adoption has been an average of 40 percent per year between 2004 and 2009" - This provides evidence of the speed of adoption. 3. "more than 90 percent of non-credit card users were unaware of safety issues existing in credit card use and more than 60 percent had little knowledge of the consequences of credit card debt." - These statistics support the argument that there's a knowledge gap. 4. "72 percent of Chinese credit card holders were never late on their payments, only 55 percent were able to pay the full balance." - These figures show different aspects of credit card usage. 5. "70 percent of Chinese credit card holders said that credit cards contributed to overspending." - This supports the idea that credit cards may lead to spending problems. Looking at how these figures are used in the text, they all serve to provide evidence for the claims being made about credit card usage in China, the problems associated with it, and the need for financial education. Now, let's consider each answer choice: (A) record the valuable data - This suggests the figures are simply being stored or documented, which isn't the main purpose in this context. (B) provide concrete evidence - This suggests the figures are used to support claims or arguments, which aligns with how the figures are used in the text. (C) introduce a research method - The text doesn't describe how the research was conducted, just presents findings. (D) give a detailed description - While the text does describe credit card usage in China, the primary purpose of the figures is to support arguments rather than just to describe.
